abstract.t: Verifiable Convergence Protocol (VCP) defines the minimal data types and APIs required for autonomous agents and human-machine systems to achieve epistemic convergence without centralized truth arbiters. VCP is transport-agnostic, blockchain-agnostic, and model-agnostic. Any system implementing VCP is an Epistemic Execution System (EES).
The key invariant: C(manipulation) > C(validation) for all Claim objects.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: As artificial intelligence transitions to autonomous agents capable of independent actions, the lack of a standardized mechanism for verifiable trust and epistemic convergence has become a critical bottleneck. VCP addresses this by providing a minimal protocol for claims, actions, and verifiable traces.
The key invariant is that the cost of manipulating a Claim's confidence exceeds the cost of validating it for all Claim objects: C(manipulation) > C(validation).

list.t: It implements the 4 Core Endpoints in Section 4.
Confidence for all Claims is computed via Multiplicative Weights Update + Gossip Consensus as defined in Section 5 (GQRVP).
All Action objects reference Claim[] as basis. Actions without basis MUST be rejected at Gateway.
Trace is immutable, content-addressed, and publicly auditable.

section.name: Claim
section.t: The atomic unit of verifiable information.

section.name: Formal Completeness Boundary
section.t: VCP operates at the boundary of formal provability. Formal-to-epistemic transition threshold: theta_critical = 0.731. When confidence >= threshold, system may treat result as formally sufficient. When below, SHOULD escalate to Continuum-style semantic verification.

Empirical sybil-10x-g1.5 trace: 30 confidence scores extracted, theta_critical 0.731 (knee point), 0 below, 30 above. Consistent with Godel-grounded design: remainder must be handled by epistemic verification.

section.name: Attestation
section.t: Cryptographic signature over a Claim by a DID.

section.name: Action
section.t: A proposed state change grounded in Claims.

section.name: Core Endpoints
section.t: All endpoints MUST be served over HTTPS. Request/response bodies are application/json.

section.name: POST /v1/claim
section.t: Submit new Claim. Request: Claim without claim_id, confidence. Response 201: {claim_id}. 400: Invalid schema.

section.name: GET /v1/claim/{claim_id}
section.t: Retrieve Claim and current confidence. 200: {claim, confidence, attestations, merkle_proof}. 404: Not found.

section.name: POST /v1/action/verify
section.t: Verify Action. Request: Action. Response 200: {status ACCEPTED|REJECTED, trace_id, reason}. Gateway MUST reject if basis empty or confidence < 0.731.

section.name: GET /v1/trace/{trace_id}
section.t: Retrieve immutable history. 200: Trace. 404: Not found.

section.name: GQRVP: Security Parameters
section.t: To prevent Sybil and amplification attacks, VCP-Compatible implementations MUST use: eta=0.2 (learning rate), gamma=1.5 (amplification), eps=0.1 (mixing). Formal proof of 67% Byzantine resilience under these parameters is given in /specs/RFC/GQRVP-security.md.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: VCP does not resolve conflicts caused by asymmetry of goals. VCP guarantees that cost of manipulating confidence exceeds cost of validating it, given honest supermajority of stake-weighted attention.

section.name: Appendix B: Godel and theta_critical
section.t: Threshold theta_critical=0.731 is practical boundary between formal sufficiency and semantic escalation, grounded in observation that no formal system can prove all true statements of interest, as per Godel's Second Incompleteness Theorem. In sybil-10x-g1.5 trace: 30 scores, threshold 0.731, 0 below, 30 above. Demonstrates stable regime where high-confidence convergence dominates, while protocol reserves formal-to-epistemic transition point.
